channel.title: Troika Games on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.
channel.description: Recent content in Troika Games on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.

item.title: Why Troika Games Were So Buggy
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about why Troika games were so buggy.

I get asked this a lot and there are a lot of articles and videos talking about how Troika games, were just so incredibly buggy. And I do have a video on why RPGs in general are so buggy and I’ll link it in the description And I will re — remind you: Troika only RPGs. We made three RPGs. It’s not why we were called Troika: Um, but the short answer is we had a lot of feature ideas. We did not edit ourselves very much at all and we were a small team and that’s just combined to make very, very buggy RPGs.

item.title: Troika's Conan The Barbarian Proposal
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about another game proposal I made.

This is back in June of 2004. I’ll talk about why I made it and what it was, but it was for. It was for the Conan, the Barbarian RPG. Um, I was doing some going through my files for reasons, and I found all kinds of interesting things, um, but one of the things I assembled across were was a cache of a bunch of proposals that we had written when vampire, the uh Masquerade bloodlines was winding down. I’ve talked about one of them, the B’s Gate 3 proposal, but this was one I totally forgot about This was entirely written by me. I will read you some of it, but let me give you a little backstory.

item.title: The Origin Of Troika's Name
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the origin of the name for troa games.

I thought I had talked about this before but I guess I haven’t, and I’ve had people ask as recently as doing yesterday’s video on what I would have speculated would have happened if troa games had still been around for Kickstarter. Theo rodk 782 asked what was the origin of tra’s name.

item.title: Troika And Kickstarter: What If?
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about troa games and Kickstarter.

No, Kickstarter did not exist when tro was there, but someone asked a question on the channel. I think it’s been asked multiple times. It’s also something I’ve thought about a lot. It’s something I’ve actually discussed a little bit. I I know I had this discussion with Leonard I talked about with a couple other designers at obsidian when I was there. But let’s dive into it. Um, nu4, Ed — I don’t know how to pronounce — that asks: if Kickstarter had been a thing while tro was alive, would you have tried to crowdfund and if yes, what? So I will give you the answers as briefly as I can and then I’ll dive into it. Yes, and Arcanum 2, so yes, if, crowdfunding had existed, troa definitely would have gone that path. Tra was a very small company, when we made arcum, we were 14 people and because of that, we were very efficient.

item.title: Late-Stage Troika
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about late stage troa, because I’ve told a lot of troa stories already and you can look them up.

There’s a whole troa playlist. I’ve talked about the development of all three of our games. I’ve talked about the flat hierarchy we tried to do. I’ve talked about how our games there didn’t have very good sales.

They’re all now cult classics, which is nice, I guess. Um, I told and I did an entire video of troa stories with me as the bad guy. That all happened during, so the reason I’m calling this late stage troa is I’m kind of focusing on about the last year.

item.title: Troika's Flat Hierarchy Experiment
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about flat management hierarchies.

I don’t think many of you realized that we did that at tra, or I should say we experimented with that at troa. It only lasted for one project, but Arcanum was created under a completely flat hierarchy. Yes, the we had. We had three owners and then all the employees. But I’ll explain. I’m going to go through exactly what I mean by flat hierarchy, but also part of the reason we had to have owners that there are legal reasons you have. You have to have someone who signs the paperwork with the lawyer, or signs the paperwork with the City of Irvine, or sign the paperwork with the county of orangee or the state of California or the federal government. You had to have someone who said, yeah, I’m in charge here for some things external to the company, internal. We were about as flat as you can be and I’ll walk through and explain. So this was. Troa from 98 to 2001. We hired only seniors.

item.title: Why I Won't Run Another Business
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to answer another question, this time from user Delta pi 8859, who asks a large number of questions, luckily about one topic.

Let me ask the questions first. What was the reason why you didn’t create a second Studio after torika games. Do you think running a business is not fun? Did it take the fun out of development? Was quality of life not as expected when doing your own company? Or to defeat the motivation of opening one in the first place because unexpected experiences? Where are the financial considerations?

item.title: Troika Memories
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I wanted to reminisce about Troika.

I’ve talked about the projects we did there — ironically three of them — Arcanum, Temple of Elemental Evil and vampire bloodlines, and I know when Leonard and I talked, it just sounded like Troika was so incredibly stressful. And it was, and I’m still trying to figure out how to talk about work-life balance versus passion. But I wanted to talk about how fun frika was, and I start off by saying, yes, I’ve worn this shirt before, but that’s because what I wanted to wear from Troika wasn’t a shirt. We were originally wanted to make something besides the shirt. We would talk about pants or shorts that had a Troika logo embroidered on them. We didn’t want to do a hat or a hoodie. I forget where the idea came from, but we had an idea, and even Sharon Shellman, um Jason’s wife and artist on Arcanum and handled our HR and finances, agreed. This was a good idea, so we made foreign boxer shorts.

item.title: Troika's Baldur's Gate 3 Proposal
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about troika’s botters Gate 3 proposal.

I found this in a folder I didn’t expect to see it in. I was looking up something about Temple of Elemental Evil to see if I could find the date of the initial proposal that we wrote, and I found Troika bg3 proposal dated July 2003.. So this would have been made before I even finished Temple, because that was finished in the fall of 2003, and before I went on to vampire. So I read through it and I thought I’d give you an idea of what we were planning to make.

item.title: Proposed Expansions to Vampire Bloodlines
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about two potential expansions — or you could say it’s SQL — that Troika had designed for Vampire: The Masquerade — Bloodlines.

I even found buried in one of them the fact that we were planning a trilogy, which I forgot, and there’s even a little note about what the third one would be. Before I talk about that, I just want to say, there have been so many comments I, I literally can’t keep up. I was trying to keep up. This is I’ve been doing this for just over two weeks. The first week I really did try to keep up. The second week I was falling behind. The last two days I’ve had a lot of client meetings. I have client meetings now.

item.title: Troika's Secret Fourth Project
item.description:
Hi everyone, it’s me Tim.

Hey, I want to talk to you about troika’s fourth project, secret project. I’m not talking demo here, I’m talking paid project that we completed. Before I can do that, let me give you a little context. So I moved to California in the late 80s from Virginia to go to graduate school at University of California, Irvine. I got my masters in 89 in computer science with a specialty in artificial intelligence, with a subspecialty in integrated learning methods. But I started on my PhD a couple years later. I went — no, not going to do this — and I went to go work at interplay. That was in 91., so in 2004, when we were finishing up vampire Bloodlines — I think it was September of 2004 — I got approached by a friend of mine who had graduated from UCI with a PhD and was working for the US Department of Defense. He had developed the system and he wanted to know if I could take tempo of Elemental Evil and write an API programming interface so that an external AI could run the game. And I was like I think I could do that and he goes, we’ll pay you.

item.title: Troika's Post Apocalyptic Demo
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim.

What you are seeing is the only known video footage of epic, which was troika’s in-house post-apocalyptic game we started designing and implementing towards the end of vampire. Uh, of course it never came out. This footage isn’t from mine.

I’ll include the link below. It’s not from my archive. I don’t have any video footage. In fact, I don’t even have any code.

I have a lot of design docs and notes, but my epic folder seems very sparse. The first document I have for this game is dated January of 2003.. And the latest one is dated June of 2004., so let me tell you how I remember this, and I’ll also tell you one thing that someone at least disagrees with me on. But so in 2003 we were contacted by interplay who told us that Bethesda was bidding on the Fallout IP. And did we want to bid? We did. This is where our memories diverge.

item.title: Troika's Might & Magic 10 Design
item.description:
Hi. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about troika’s proposal from might Magic 10..

I’ll be honest, I’ve forgotten we had done this, but recently I did an interview with RAF colantonio and Peter salnikov on their YouTube channel, House of the dev — I’ll put a link in the description — and Ralph said that he always felt bad that Arcane was pitted up against Troika for might Magic 10.. You know, and I told him — I don’t even know if I knew that, but I told him, I mean, Publishers do that all the time. They like you know developers to compete for things. And I told him not to worry about it. We didn’t get it, but I went on to work on vampire anyway. This occurred right after Temple shipped.

item.title: Troika Games' Lord of the Rings Demo (2001)
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me Tim.

After talking about Arcanum 2 Journey to the Center of Arcanum yesterday, it reminded me that after we worked on the sequel and it got rejected, Troika actually worked on Lord of the Rings, so I’m going to show you that demo in just a second, but I thought I’d give you some context. So when the sequel idea got rejected, Sarah approached us with Lord of the Rings, they just gotten the rights to the book. Now the movies had come out, they didn’t have any movie rights, but they had rights to the book and they wanted to know if we had any ideas.

item.title: Arcanum 2: Journey to the Center of Arcanum
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the sequel to Arcanum called Journey to the Center of Arcanum.

So let me give you some background. Um, for those of you new, Troika games was a game Studio that I started with Leonard barski and Jason Anderson on April 1st 1998, and our first game that we shipped was Arcanum. Arcanum shipped in 2001 using a new isometric engine we made. However, even before it shipped, we started thinking about ideas for sequels and that we wanted to make the jump to 3D. And we were talking to valve, because the VP of valve, Scott Lynch, had been the VP who signed us to Sierra for Arcanum and we were talking with them about their Source engine and we really wanted to use it. So we put together a design dock, which I still have. I read through and I’m like: Whoa, We were very ambitious. Uh, Troika liked to do a lot of things. In fact, Arcana might commonly refer to as our kitchen.
